home.title: Wepisia
home.quote: « Knowledge is like a baobab tree: no single person can embrace it. »
home.twoByTwoDesc: Contingency analysis, odds ratio and relative risk.
home.biostatisticsDesc: From power calculations to Mantel-Haenszel tests, access a suite of scientifically validated tools.
home.aiDescription: Automatically interpret your results with our contextual intelligence engine.
home.geospatialDescription: Visualize your epidemiological events on interactive maps with dynamic heatmaps and intelligent clusters.
home.simulationDescription: Simulate complex epidemiological scenarios with our advanced simulation engine at optimal performance.

settings.title: Settings
settings.customizeApp: Customize the application to your preferences
settings.reduceAnimationsDescription: Disables or simplifies transitions and animations
settings.highContrastDescription: Increases color contrast for better readability
settings.compactModeDescription: Reduces margins and padding to display more content
settings.enableNotificationsDescription: Show toast notifications in the application
settings.soundNotificationsDescription: Play a sound when a notification appears
settings.hapticFeedbackDescription: Vibrate the device on notifications (if supported)
settings.settingsSaved: Settings are automatically saved in your browser.

stdMortalityCalculator.title: SMR Calculator
stdMortalityCalculator.subtitle: Epidemiology and mortality statistics.
stdMortalityCalculator.footnote: * For small counts (observed ≤ 5), prefer exact methods (Fisher, Mid-P).
stdMortalityCalculator.jstatMissing: jStat not detected — exact intervals and p-values replaced by approximations.
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationNotSignif: The difference is not statistically significant (CI contains 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.reportFooter: SMR Calculator - Statistical tool for epidemiology
stdMortalityCalculator.helpPrinciple: The SMR (Standardized Mortality Ratio) compares observed deaths in a specific population to expected deaths if that population had the same mortality rates as the general population.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpCi: The CI indicates the reliability of the result. If it contains the value 1, the result is not statistically significant.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpExactDesc: Based on the Poisson distribution and the χ² law. Reference for small counts.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpMidPDesc: Continuity correction of the exact test, less conservative.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpByarDesc: Cubic approximation by Byar (Rothman & Boice, 1979), very accurate even for O > 5.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpChi2Desc: (O-E)²/E, compares fit to the Poisson distribution.

introduction.subtitle: Complete guide to biostatistics and simulation modules.

stdMortalityCalculator.title: SMR Calculator
stdMortalityCalculator.subtitle: Epidemiology and mortality statistics.
stdMortalityCalculator.footnote: * For small counts (observed ≤ 5), prefer exact methods (Fisher, Mid-P).
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationNotSignif: The difference is not statistically significant (CI contains 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.helpPrinciple: The Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) is the ratio of the number of observed deaths in the study population to the number of deaths theoretically expected if that population had the same mortality rates as the reference population.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpCi: A CI that does not include 1.0 indicates a statistically significant difference at the chosen level.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpExactDesc: Garwood (1936) exact CI based on χ² law. Bilateral exact Poisson test. OpenEpi reference method.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpMidPDesc: Lancaster (1961) Mid-P test. Less conservative than the exact test. CI calculated by numerical bisection.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpByarDesc: Cubic approximation by Byar (Rothman & Boice, 1979). Reliable for obs ≥ 1. p-value by CI inversion.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpVdbDesc: Square root transformation by Vandenbroucke (1982). p-value: z = 2(√obs − √exp).
stdMortalityCalculator.helpRgDesc: Log-normal CI: exp(ln(SMR) ± z/√obs). Rothman & Greenland (1998). Undefined for obs = 0.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpChi2Desc: Pearson χ² test (1 df). No CI provided (consistent with OpenEpi).
stdMortalityCalculator.reportFooter: Automatically generated report — for epidemiological purposes only.

twoByTwoTable.title: 2×2 Tables
twoByTwoTable.description: Assessment of the association between exposure and disease.
twoByTwoTable.explanation: 2×2 tables cross an exposure (yes/no) with a disease (yes/no). Statistics produced include Fisher's and mid-p exact tests, chi-squares, odds ratio, relative risk, risk difference and attributable fractions.

proportions.title: Proportions
proportions.description: Estimation of a proportion and its confidence interval.
proportions.exactClopperPearson: • Exact (Clopper-Pearson): Conservative, based on the binomial distribution
proportions.midPExact: • Mid-P exact: Less conservative than Clopper-Pearson
proportions.useCaseDescription: Out of 500 people tested, 75 are positive. What is the true prevalence?

proportionsSample.title: Sample Size for a Proportion
proportionsSample.description: Calculates the sample size needed to estimate a proportion.
proportionsSample.explanation: This module determines the number of subjects required to estimate a proportion with a given precision.
proportionsSample.exampleDescription: To estimate a prevalence of 20% with a precision of ±3% and a 95% confidence level:

oneRate.title: One Rate Test
oneRate.description: Inference on an incidence or mortality rate.
oneRate.explanation: Compares an observed rate to a reference rate or calculates its confidence interval. Uses the Poisson distribution for rare events.
oneRate.exampleDescription: In a cohort of 10,000 persons followed for 5 years, 25 cases are observed.

compareTwoRates.title: Comparison of Two Rates
compareTwoRates.description: Test of equality of two incidence rates.
compareTwoRates.explanation: Compares two rates based on person-time data. Calculates the rate ratio and rate difference with their confidence intervals, as well as the Mantel-Haenszel test.

anova.title: One-Way ANOVA
anova.description: Comparison of means of three or more groups.
anova.explanation: ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) tests the null hypothesis that the means of several groups are equal. It decomposes total variance into between-group and within-group variance.
anova.anovaTable: ANOVA table: Sum of squares, degrees of freedom, mean squares

tTest.title: Student's t-Test
tTest.explanation: The t-test compares the means of two independent groups. Two versions are provided: Student's t-test (equal variances) and Welch's t-test (unequal variances).
tTest.hartleyFTest: Hartley F test to compare variances

meanConfidenceInterval.title: Confidence Interval for a Mean
meanConfidenceInterval.description: Estimation of the population mean from a sample.
meanConfidenceInterval.explanation: Calculates the confidence interval for a mean from the descriptive statistics of the sample (n, mean, standard deviation). Uses the Student t distribution.

meanDifferencePower.title: Power for a Mean Difference
meanDifferencePower.description: Calculates the power of a mean comparison test.
meanDifferencePower.explanation: Power is the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is false. This module calculates it from sample sizes, the expected difference and variability.

meanDifferenceSample.title: Sample Size for Mean Difference
meanDifferenceSample.description: Determines the number of subjects needed.
meanDifferenceSample.explanation: Calculates the sample size required to detect a given difference between two means, with specified power and confidence level.

medianPercentileCi.title: Confidence Interval for Median and Percentiles
medianPercentileCi.explanation: Calculates confidence intervals for the median and percentiles (25th, 75th, etc.) using the binomial or rank-based method.

unmatchedCaseControl.title: Unmatched Case-Control Analysis
unmatchedCaseControl.description: Analysis of exposure-disease association in case-control studies.
unmatchedCaseControl.explanation: This module analyses data from an unmatched case-control study. It calculates the odds ratio (OR), its 95% confidence interval and performs the Mantel-Haenszel χ² test to test the association.

sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.title: Sample Size (Unmatched Case-Control)
sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.description: Calculates the required number of cases and controls.
sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.explanation: This module calculates the sample size for an unmatched case-control study. Users enter the desired confidence level, power, proportion of exposure in controls, and either an odds ratio or the proportion of exposure in cases.

matchedCaseControl.title: Matched Case-Control Analysis
matchedCaseControl.explanation: For matched case-control studies, this module calculates the Mantel-Haenszel odds ratio, confidence interval and McNemar test. It handles 1:1, 1:n and M:N matching.

cohort.title: Cohort Study Analysis
cohort.description: Calculation of relative risk, risk difference and attributable fractions.
cohort.explanation: In cohort studies, exposed and unexposed groups are followed over time. This module calculates the relative risk (RR), risk difference (RD), and attributable fractions in exposed and in the population.

rct.title: Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)
rct.description: Analysis specific to randomized clinical trials.
rct.explanation: Module dedicated to randomized trials. Calculates relative risk, absolute risk reduction (ARR), number needed to treat (NNT), and corresponding confidence intervals.

screening.title: Diagnostic Test Evaluation
screening.description: Sensitivity, specificity, predictive values, likelihood ratios.
screening.explanation: This module evaluates the performance of a diagnostic test against a gold standard. It calculates sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative predictive values, likelihood ratios, Youden's index, and Cohen's Kappa coefficient.

doseResponse.title: Dose-Response (Trend χ²)
doseResponse.description: Linear trend test for more than two exposure levels.
doseResponse.explanation: This module performs the Mantel-Haenszel linear trend χ² test for data with more than two exposure levels (e.g., increasing doses). It calculates the p-value at 1 degree of freedom to test the hypothesis of a linear relationship between exposure and risk.
doseResponse.multipleStrata: Multiple strata can be entered to control for confounding factors.

rByCTable.title: R×C Table
rByCTable.description: Analysis of contingency tables of any size.
rByCTable.explanation: This module generalizes 2×2 table analysis to tables of dimension R (rows) × C (columns). It calculates Pearson's χ², likelihood ratio (G²), contingency coefficient, and Cramér's V. It can also analyse multiple strata.
rByCTable.associationBloodGroupDisease: Association between blood group and disease (3 groups × 4 phenotypes)

randomNumbers.title: Random Number Generator
randomNumbers.description: Generation of random numbers for randomization and simulations.
randomNumbers.explanation: This module generates random numbers according to different distributions: uniform, normal, binomial, Poisson. It can also generate sequences for clinical trial randomization (permuted blocks).

simulation.description: Simulate the spread of an infectious disease between interconnected regions, with compartmental models and interventions.
simulation.explanation1: This module simulates the evolution of an epidemic on a network of regions (e.g., countries, districts). Each region has its own population and compartments (S, E, I, R, D, Q depending on the model chosen). Regions are connected by links that model population flows (mobility), influencing inter-regional dynamics.
simulation.explanation2: Calculations use discrete-time ordinary differential equations (ODE). The user can adjust epidemiological parameters, add interventions (lockdown, vaccination) and observe the impact in real time on an interactive map, charts, or a dynamic network.
simulation.sir: SIR: S, I, R — Basic model without incubation period
simulation.seir: SEIR: S, E, I, R — Adds an exposure phase (E)
simulation.seird: SEIRD: S, E, I, R, D — Includes mortality (D)
simulation.seiqrd: SEIQRD: S, E, I, Q, R, D — Adds quarantine (Q)
simulation.interactiveMap: Interactive map: 2D (Leaflet) or 3D (Globe) with markers proportional to population and mobility flows.
simulation.evolutionCurves: Evolution curves: Tracking of S, E, I, R, D compartments over time. Ability to compare two simulations.
simulation.dynamicGraph: Dynamic graph: Force-directed visualization, with nodes colored by infection rate, links weighted by mobility.
simulation.detailedData: Detailed data: Complete list of regions with their compartment counts and infection rates.
simulation.massVaccination: Mass vaccination: β reduced, 40% efficacy at D30
simulation.lateIntervention: Late intervention: lockdown at D50, R₀=3.0
simulation.map: Map: Type: 2D (Leaflet) / 3D (Globe) | Theme: light, dark, satellite | Marker shape: circle / icon | Line opacity (mobility)
simulation.graphs: Charts: Line style: solid / dashed | Adjustable thickness | Fill opacity under curves | Grid show/hide | Intervention annotations
simulation.network: Network: Node shape: circle, square, triangle | Link style: solid / dashed | Repulsion force (charge) | Region clustering (based on cluster attribute)
simulation.step1: Select a model (SEIRD by default) and a scenario (Base).
simulation.step2: Click Start to launch the simulation. Observe the evolution on the map and charts.
simulation.step3: Add an intervention (lockdown) via the dedicated button to see its impact on the infected curve.
simulation.step4: Switch to comparison mode to simulate an alternative scenario with different parameters and compare the curves.
simulation.keyboardShortcuts: Keyboard shortcuts (3D map mode): arrows to rotate the globe, R to recenter, 2/3 to change projection.
simulation.technicalNote: Note: The simulation uses a discrete time step of 1 day. Equations are based on standard compartmental models. Inter-region mobility is modelled by a diffusion term proportional to the difference in prevalence.

geospatial.description: Based on Leaflet and react-leaflet — explore your epidemiological data on dynamic maps.
geospatial.explanation1: The geospatial module allows loading, visualizing and analysing georeferenced data (points, incidents, incidence rates…). It offers three complementary display modes: individual markers, clustering for large volumes, and heatmap to visualize densities.
geospatial.explanation2: Data can be imported from CSV or Excel files, or via built-in examples (historical outbreaks, continental data). An integrated AI analysis automatically provides summaries, trends and alerts.
geospatial.dataImport: Data import: Drag-and-drop or browse your files. Automatic detection of latitude/longitude columns and intensity value.
geospatial.multiLayers: Multi-layers: Each dataset is an independent layer. Show/hide, change color, radius or opacity of each series.
geospatial.integratedAIAnalysis: Integrated AI analysis: Automatically generate a summary, trends, recommendations and risk alerts from visible data.
geospatial.individualPoints: Individual points: Each data point is represented by a circle whose size and color vary by intensity (e.g., number of cases). Ideal for small datasets.
geospatial.pointsParameters: Parameters: global radius, opacity, color per dataset
geospatial.clustering: Clustering: Automatically groups nearby points into clusters whose size indicates the number of elements. Recommended for more than 500 points.
geospatial.clusteringOptions: Options: cluster color, text color, max cluster radius, deactivation threshold on zoom
geospatial.heatmap: Heatmap: Visualizes point density weighted by intensity. Ideal for identifying clusters or risk areas.
geospatial.heatmapSettings: Settings: radius, blur, color gradient, minimum opacity
geospatial.heatmapConfig: Heatmap: Radius — heat point size (default 25) | Blur — gradient smoothing (default 15) | Minimum opacity — transparency of cold areas | Customizable gradient — e.g.: 0.4 → blue, 1.0 → red
geospatial.clusteringConfig: Clustering: Cluster color and text color | Max cluster radius (grouping distance) | Deactivation threshold — from which zoom level clusters display as individual points | Spiderfy — spider layout at maximum zoom
geospatial.note: Note: Each dataset retains its own visibility, clustering, heatmap, color, radius and opacity settings. Global parameters serve as defaults.
geospatial.summary: Summary: Description of clusters, correlations, global trends
geospatial.insights: Insights: Key facts: risk area, increase, under-reporting
geospatial.alerts: Alerts: Risk level (low / moderate / high) and points of attention
geospatial.step1: Load a CSV file containing latitude, longitude and cases columns.
geospatial.step2: Enable clustering to visualize the global distribution, then switch to heatmap to identify hotspots.
geospatial.step3: In the configuration panel, adjust the dataset color, point radius and opacity.
geospatial.step4: Click Analyze to get an AI report with risk level and recommendations.

workspace.description: Run R code directly in your browser.
workspace.explanation1: The workspace embeds a complete R engine based on WebR (R ported to WebAssembly). You can create, open and save multiple .R scripts, run all code or just a single line, and immediately visualize results in the console or as plots.
workspace.explanation2: The following features are available: script execution, file management, interactive console, plot capture, image download, installation of common packages (ggplot2, dplyr, …). Some parts (advanced animation, full package support) are still being improved and marked as beta.
workspace.multiFileEditor: Multi-file editor: Tabs to manage multiple scripts simultaneously. Rename, duplicate, delete via context menu. Auto-save in browser (localStorage).
workspace.interactiveConsoleFeature: Interactive console: Enter live R commands, navigate history with arrows, visualize text outputs and plots in the same area.
workspace.graphicalVisualizations: Graphical visualizations: All plots generated by R are automatically captured and displayed. Grid mode, animation mode, individual download and full-screen zoom.
workspace.packageManagement: Package management: Install packages from the webR repository (e.g., ggplot2, dplyr, KernSmooth) via the dedicated field in the header or directly with webr::install().
workspace.animationBetaDetail: Animation (beta): If your script produces multiple plots (e.g., in a loop), you can view them in grid mode or launch an animation. The button allows play/pause. Manual navigation is also possible.
workspace.animationNote: Animation works for pre-generated sequences, but continuous rendering of dynamic plots is still being optimized.
workspace.packageInstallationDetail: Package installation: WebR allows installing packages pre-compiled for WebAssembly. In the header, a dedicated field lets you quickly install a package (e.g., ggplot2). Installation logs appear in the console.
workspace.packageNote: Packages requiring system dependencies or native compilation may not be available.
workspace.codeExample:
# Moving epidemic curve
x <- seq(-5, 10, length.out = 200)
for (i in 1:40) {
  mu <- i / 8
  y <- dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = 1.5)
  plot(x, y, type="n", ylim=c(0, 0.3), axes=FALSE)
  polygon(c(x, rev(x)), c(y, rep(0, 200)), col="#3b82f622", border=NA)
  lines(x, y, col="#3b82f6", lwd=3)
  Sys.sleep(0.04)
}
workspace.step3: Click the Run button to execute the full script. An animation will appear in the console.
workspace.step4: Use the navigation arrows or button to control the animation. Export an image by clicking the icon.

explorer.description: Access more than 35 million biomedical references directly from your workspace.
explorer.explanation1: The explorer integrates a complete interface to the PubMed database (NCBI). You can search by keywords, filter by year and publication type, sort by relevance or date, and view detailed abstracts.
explorer.explanation2: A unique MeSH query generation feature uses PubMed's automatic translation to enrich your search terms with appropriate MeSH descriptors. Articles can be saved locally (favorites) and exported in RIS format for your reference management software.
explorer.advancedSearch: Advanced search: Keywords, filters by period (e.g., 2010-2025), article types (review, clinical trial, meta-analysis…), sort by relevance or publication date.
explorer.meshGenerator: MeSH generator: From a free expression, retrieve the MeSH translation proposed by PubMed, including controlled terms and field tags (e.g., "[PTYP]").
explorer.favoritesExport: Favorites & export: Save up to 200 articles locally. Export a page of results or all favorites in RIS format (compatible with EndNote, Zotero, Mendeley).
explorer.home: Home: Search bar with filter | Direct access to favorites from the link at the bottom | Display of MeSH suggestions in a separate panel
explorer.results: Results: Quick toggle between search and favorites | Side panel with article details (abstract, authors, DOI, PMID) | Temporal distribution chart of results
explorer.step1: In centered mode, type covid-19 vaccine.
explorer.step2: Click Generate MeSH to get an optimized query (e.g., "COVID-19 Vaccines"[Mesh]). Apply it.
explorer.step4: Browse results, click an article to see details. Add it to favorites (bookmark icon).
explorer.step5: Switch to the Favorites tab, then Export all to generate a RIS file.

analysis.title: Epidemiological AI Analysis
analysis.description: Smart analysis will be available soon — automatic insights, cluster detection and epidemiological recommendations.

settings.timelineDescription: Temporal progression slider — real data to connect.
settings.comparisonNoDatasets: Load multiple datasets to compare them.
settings.comparisonHint: Load multiple examples then compare their indicators.
settings.realtimeDescription: Will simulate receiving live data from a surveillance API.

map.emptyDescription: Import a CSV/Excel file or load an epidemiological example via the "Examples" button.

modal.title: Epidemiological examples
modal.description: Real documented data — WHO, Johns Hopkins, CEREMUJER and other organizations.

toasts.formatError: Unsupported format — CSV or Excel only

empty_state.title: Start a simulation
empty_state.description: Choose a real disease example or a scenario to launch the simulation and visualize epidemic spread.

charts.empty: Run a simulation to see the charts.

data_modal.title: Import data
data_modal.csv_label: CSV — regions (columns: id, name, population, S, E, I, R, D, latitude, longitude, color, connections, cluster)

explanation_modal.title: Documentation
explanation_modal.slider_body: Drag the D0-D365 slider to visualize the epidemic state on any given day. The map and dashboard update instantly. Use Play to animate the progression.
explanation_modal.models_body: SIR, SEIR, SEIRD, SEIQRD. The full trajectory is precompiled on parameter change — no flickering during playback.
explanation_modal.rt_body: Rt (effective reproduction number) is calculated as R0 × S/N × (1 − reductions). Rt < 1 means the epidemic is under control.
explanation_modal.phase_body: The S-I chart traces the trajectory in S × I space. The characteristic arc shape is the signature of a well-parameterized epidemic model.
explanation_modal.interventions_body: Each intervention reduces β by a percentage, applied from the start day. Effects are cumulative (capped at 95%).
explanation_modal.comparison_body: The "Compare" button launches a parallel simulation with different parameters. Curves are displayed as overlays.

examples_modal.title: Disease examples
examples_modal.subtitle: Parameters and initial conditions inspired by real data

covid19.name: COVID-19
covid19.description: Global pandemic. Airborne transmission, incubation period 5-6 days, CFR ~1%.

ebola2014.name: Ebola Outbreak
ebola2014.description: Major health crisis in West Africa. CFR ~50%. Transmission by direct contact.

flu_seasonal.name: Seasonal Flu
flu_seasonal.description: Classic winter epidemic. Moderate R0, fast recovery, low general lethality.

measles.name: Measles
measles.description: Highly contagious disease (R0 12-18). Effective vaccination but insufficient coverage in some areas.

meningitis.name: Meningococcal Meningitis
meningitis.description: Seasonal epidemics in the sub-Saharan "meningitis belt". Lethality ~10%.

cholera.name: Cholera
cholera.description: Outbreaks linked to contaminated water. Rapid mortality without treatment, low with rehydration.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.subtitle: Wepisia
privacy.summary: Wepisia is an open-source epidemiology and biostatistics tool. This policy explains how we handle your data.
privacy.noDataDesc: Wepisia does not collect, store or transmit any personal data. All calculations are performed locally in your browser.
privacy.localDesc: Data entered (table values, simulation parameters) remains on your device and is cleared when the tab is closed.
privacy.noTrackingDesc: No third-party cookies, no analytics scripts, no advertising. Your privacy is fully respected.
privacy.clearLocalStorage: You can clear this data at any time via your browser settings.
privacy.dataUsageDesc: The only data you enter is used for calculations.
privacy.calculationsDesc: Values you enter (e.g., counts, parameters) are only used to generate the displayed results. They are never transmitted to our servers.
privacy.persistenceDesc: No data is retained after the tab is closed. You can also manually clear stored preferences.
privacy.cookiesDesc: The application does not use cookies.
privacy.cookiesDetail: Only local storage (localStorage) may be used to remember your language preferences and interface state. This data is not a cookie and is never shared.
privacy.thirdPartiesDesc: No information is shared with third parties.
privacy.thirdPartiesDetail: The application does not contain external scripts (analytics, advertising) and does not transmit any data. The only network requests are for loading resources needed to operate (JavaScript libraries, stylesheets).
privacy.securityDesc: Your data remains under your control.
privacy.localComputationDesc: All calculations are performed on your machine. No data leaves your browser.
privacy.httpsDesc: The site is served over HTTPS, guaranteeing the integrity of downloaded files.
privacy.changesDesc: Any changes will be published on this page.
privacy.changesDetail: This policy may be updated occasionally. The last update date appears at the top of the page. We invite you to check it regularly.
privacy.contactDesc: For any questions regarding this policy.

about.title: About
about.subtitle: Wepisia
about.intro: A free tool for epidemiology, designed for accessibility in low-resource countries
about.visionDesc: In developing countries, resources are often limited. Wepisia was designed to address these realities: local operation, no installation, multilingualism, and free code (MIT).
about.openEpiDesc: OpenEpi revolutionized access to epidemiological calculations by making them free, online and accessible to all. Published under the MIT license, it remains a global reference.
about.commitmentDesc: Wepisia is and will remain free, open source (MIT) and multilingual. Developed by Xcept-Health, it embodies our conviction: public health tools must be accessible without barriers.
about.openEpiHeritageDesc: Context, objectives and characteristics of the original project.
about.openEpiContext: OpenEpi was born from the observation that commercial software did not always meet the needs of epidemiologists. The team wanted to provide modern, documented and tested programs, using a common interface, running via the Web or locally, platform-independent, and open source.
about.openEpiGoals: Objectives: provide modern, platform-independent, open-source programs, facilitate interaction between epidemiologists and developers.
about.feature1: Stratified tables, screening tests, diagnostic method evaluation
about.feature2: Power calculations for cohort studies, case-control, clinical trials
about.feature3: Random number generator, trend χ² test (dose-response)
about.feature4: Results file saving and sample data loading
about.genesisText: Initiated by F. Ariel Shadrac OUEDRAOGO, a Burkinabè medical student and self-taught developer, Wepisia is an African evolution of OpenEpi. Completely redesigned with modern technologies (React, Vite, Tailwind), it brings:
about.featureSimulation: Advanced multi-region simulation (SIR, SEIR, SEIRD, SEIQRD)
about.featureMultilingual: Full multilingualism (Mooré, Wolof, Hausa, Swahili, French, English)
about.recognitionDesc: We wish to express our deep gratitude to the creators of OpenEpi
about.otherContributors: and all other contributors to the project
about.recognitionConclusion: Without their pioneering work, Wepisia would never have come to life. Their source code, available under the MIT license, remains the scientific foundation on which our project rests.
about.contributeDesc: Developers, epidemiologists, translators, designers: all contributions are welcome!

terms.title: Terms of Use
terms.subtitle: Wepisia
terms.summary: Please read these terms carefully before using Wepisia. By accessing or using the application, you agree to be bound by these terms.
terms.freeAndOpenDesc: Wepisia is provided free of charge under the MIT license. You are free to use, modify and distribute the code.
terms.openSourceDesc: The complete source code is available on GitHub, guaranteeing full transparency and the possibility of community contributions.
terms.noWarrantyDesc: The software is provided "as is", without any warranty. Use at your own risk.
terms.acceptanceDesc: By using Wepisia, you accept these terms.
terms.acceptanceDetail: These terms of use govern your use of the Wepisia application, including all content, features and services. If you do not accept any of these terms, you must not use the application.
terms.useDesc: Wepisia is intended for educational, research and professional purposes in epidemiology and biostatistics.
terms.permittedTeaching: Public health training and capacity building
terms.prohibitedCommercial: Resale or commercial redistribution without authorization
terms.prohibitedMisuse: Use likely to harm the service or interfere with others' use
terms.prohibitedIllegal: Any illegal activity or violation of applicable laws
terms.ipDetail: The source code is under the MIT license. You are free to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense and/or sell copies of the software, subject to the terms of the MIT license. The names "Wepisia", "Xcept-Health" and associated logos are trademarks of Xcept-Health.
terms.licenseNotice: MIT License - see the LICENSE file in the repository for more details.
terms.disclaimerDetail: Wepisia is provided without any warranty, express or implied, including but not limited to warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose or non-infringement. The entire risk as to the quality and performance of the software lies with you.
terms.disclaimerMedical: Medical disclaimer: Wepisia is a statistical analysis and simulation tool. It does not provide medical advice. All results must be interpreted by qualified professionals. The creators cannot be held responsible for clinical decisions made on the basis of results.
terms.liabilityDetail: In no event shall Xcept-Health or its contributors be liable for any claim, damage or other liability arising from the use of Wepisia, whether in contract, tort or otherwise.

notFound.title: Lost in the void?
notFound.message: This page has drifted off our radar. Use your instruments to return to port.
notFound.modalText: These coordinates lead to the Monument des Martyrs in Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso — a memorial to those who sacrificed their lives for freedom. A place of memory and hope.

twoByTwo.title: 2×2 Table
twoByTwo.description: Calculate the odds ratio, relative risk and chi-square tests.
twoByTwo.increasedRisk: The relative risk indicates increased risk in exposed.
twoByTwo.protectiveEffect: The relative risk indicates a protective effect of exposure.
twoByTwo.noAssociation: The relative risk equals 1, suggesting no association.
twoByTwo.ciNote: CIs for OR and RR are calculated by Taylor approximation (Woolf method).
twoByTwo.orNote: CI calculated by Taylor series (Woolf method). PAF|OR = (a/c₁)·(OR−1)/OR.
twoByTwo.principleText: 2×2 table analysis studies the association between an exposure and a disease. The odds ratio (case-control studies) and relative risk (cohort studies) are calculated, along with various independence tests.
twoByTwo.fisherDesc: Based on the hypergeometric distribution, recommended for small counts.
twoByTwo.interpretationText: If the confidence interval of OR or RR excludes 1, the association is significant. The p-value of the χ² test indicates the significance of the association (p < 0.05).

doseResponse.title: Dose-Response Analysis
doseResponse.description: Linear trend test for different exposure levels.
doseResponse.reportFooter: Dose-Response Calculator - Statistical tool for epidemiology
doseResponse.principleText: This test evaluates whether there is a linear trend between the exposure level (dose) and the risk of disease. It is used in case-control or cohort studies with several ordered exposure levels.
doseResponse.methodsText: For each level, the odds ratio and relative risk are calculated relative to the reference level (lowest). The trend test is an extension of the χ² test (Mantel), with a statistic distributed as χ² with 1 degree of freedom.
doseResponse.interpretationText: A p-value < 0.05 indicates a significant trend. Direction (positive/negative) indicates whether risk increases or decreases with dose.

rByC.title: R×C Table
rByC.description: Analysis of contingency tables of arbitrary size.
rByC.significantInterpretation: The p-value of {{p}} indicates a significant association between variables (p < 0.05). Cramér's V of {{v}} measures the strength of this association.
rByC.nonSignificantInterpretation: The p-value of {{p}} does not indicate a significant association between variables (p ≥ 0.05). Cramér's V of {{v}} suggests a weak or non-existent association.
rByC.jStatError: jStat not available — calculations are disabled
rByC.principleText: Pearson's chi-square (χ²) test is used to determine whether there is a statistically significant association between two categorical variables. It compares observed frequencies in an R×C contingency table to theoretical frequencies expected under the hypothesis of independence.
rByC.independenceDesc: The two variables are not related.
rByC.dependenceDesc: There is an association between them.
rByC.expectedCountsDesc: Less than 20% of cells should have an expected count below 5, and no expected count should be below 1. If this condition is not met, consider an exact test (e.g., Fisher's test for 2×2 tables).
rByC.unpairedDataDesc: Each subject contributes to a single cell.
rByC.chiSquareDesc: Sum of (observed - expected)² / expected. The higher the value, the greater the deviation from independence.
rByC.dfDesc: (number of rows - 1) × (number of columns - 1). They determine the shape of the χ² distribution under H₀.
rByC.pValueDesc: Probability of observing a χ² value as extreme (or more) under H₀. If p < 0.05 (usual threshold), H₀ is rejected at 5% risk.
rByC.cramersVDesc: Measure of association strength, ranging from 0 (independence) to 1 (perfect dependence).
rByC.complementaryMethodsDesc: For 2×2 tables, other tests may be more appropriate:
rByC.fisherExactDesc: Recommended when expected counts are low (expected < 5).
rByC.yatesCorrectionDesc: Sometimes applied to χ² for 2×2 tables, it makes the test more conservative.
rByC.jStatNote: This calculator uses the jStat library to obtain exact p-values from the χ² distribution.
rByC.exampleText: Suppose we study the relationship between smoking (smoker / non-smoker) and the occurrence of lung cancer (yes / no). A 2×2 table could be:
rByC.exampleInterpretation: The χ² test would give a p-value < 0.001, indicating a significant association. Cramér's V would be approximately 0.45, indicating a moderate strength.

screening.title: Screening
screening.description: Analysis of screening test performance at multiple levels.
screening.rocTextApprox: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve — text approximation
screening.rocLabel: TPR = true positive rate, FPR = false positive rate
screening.helpPrincipleText: This module faithfully reproduces OpenEpi's "Screening Test" tool. It evaluates the performance of a diagnostic or screening test at multiple levels (ordinal or continuous). For each cutoff threshold, it calculates:
screening.helpEntropy: It also provides level-specific likelihood ratios and the area under the ROC curve (AUC) with its confidence interval.
screening.helpAucHighDesc: Performing test — acceptable to excellent discrimination
screening.helpAucLowDesc: Poorly informative test — close to chance
screening.helpConfidenceText: All intervals are calculated according to recognized methods, identical to OpenEpi.
screening.helpRocDesc: Points are generated by cumulating counts from most pathological to least pathological level. AUC is calculated by the trapezoidal method.
screening.helpLevelLRDesc: LR = (casesᵢ / non-casesᵢ) / (total cases / total non-cases). CI based on standard error of log(LR) with Katz formula.
screening.helpKappaMethodDesc: Measure of agreement beyond chance. CI = κ ± 1.96·SE(κ).
screening.helpEntropyMethodDesc: Percentage reduction in uncertainty (Shannon entropy) after a positive or negative test.
screening.helpBiasMethodDesc: (TP + FP - FN - TN) / N. Reflects the overall classification imbalance.
screening.helpExampleText: Suppose a blood glucose test for diagnosing diabetes. Patients are classified into five glycemia levels (1 = normal, 5 = very high). The entered data might be:
screening.helpExampleInterpretation: The analysis produces a ROC curve, an AUC of 0.5326 (95% CI: 0.3728 - 0.6925), and for each threshold the sensitivities, specificities, etc. The best tradeoff can thus be chosen.
screening.helpOrderNoteText: Levels must be entered from least pathological (first row) to most pathological (last row). This is essential for a correct ROC curve.

oneRate.title: Rate 1
oneRate.description: Analysis of a rate and its confidence interval - PersonTime1
oneRate.interpretationText: The observed rate is {{rate}} event(s) per person-time unit. The {{level}}% confidence interval (Fisher method) ranges from {{lower}} to {{upper}}.{{precision}}
oneRate.principleText: The incidence rate (or incidence density) measures the speed at which events occur in a population followed for a certain duration. It is calculated as the number of new cases divided by the sum of participation times (person-time). Its confidence interval reflects the precision of this estimate.
oneRate.ciText: The 95% confidence interval (by default) indicates the range of values in which the true rate lies with 95% probability. The narrower the interval, the more precise the estimate. If the interval contains the value 1, one cannot conclude an excess or deficit of risk compared to a reference population (in the case of an external comparison).
oneRate.midpDesc: Continuity correction of the exact Poisson test, less conservative than Fisher's exact test. Recommended for small counts.
oneRate.fisherDesc: Based on the χ² distribution, it is the reference method for small numbers. It guarantees a type I error risk at most equal to the nominal threshold.
oneRate.normalDesc: Valid for large counts (generally a > 20). Uses the standard error √a / N.
oneRate.byarDesc: Very accurate even for moderate counts (a > 5). Based on a transformation of the Poisson parameter.
oneRate.rothmanDesc: Uses the logarithm of the rate and its standard error (1/√a). The interval is then exponential. Ensures the lower bound remains positive.
oneRate.exampleText: In a cohort study, 33 cancer cases are observed in workers followed for 22 person-years. The incidence rate is:
oneRate.exampleInterpretation: The 95% confidence interval by Fisher's exact method is [1.03 - 2.10] (rounded). This means the true rate has a 95% chance of being between 1.03 and 2.10 events per person-year.

twoRates.title: TwoRates
twoRates.description: Comparison of two incidence rates - Ratio, difference, CI (PersonTime2, OpenEpi)
twoRates.copyPrefix: Comparison of two rates - OpenEpi PersonTime2
twoRates.errorNoCases: At least one group must have cases.
twoRates.zScoreTitle: z-Score and Exact Measures of Association
twoRates.interpretationHigher: The rate in group 1 is {{percent}}% higher than in group 2.
twoRates.interpretationLower: The rate in group 1 is {{percent}}% lower than in group 2.
twoRates.helpPrincipleText: This module reproduces OpenEpi's PersonTime2 tool. It compares two incidence rates (cases / person-time) by calculating the rate ratio (RR), rate difference and their confidence intervals by several methods (exact and approximate).
twoRates.helpMidp: Miettinen (1974d): continuity correction of the exact test, recommended.
twoRates.helpFisher: Armitage (1971): based on conditional hypergeometric distribution.

meanCi.title: Confidence Interval for the Mean
meanCi.copyPrefix: Confidence interval for the mean - OpenEpi MeanCI
meanCi.interpretationText: At {{level}}% confidence, the true population mean lies between {{lower}} and {{upper}} (recommended t method).
meanCi.fpcApplied: A finite population correction was applied (FPC = {{fpc}}).
meanCi.referencesNote: Methods based on OpenEpi - MeanCI. Z interval: normal approximation. t interval: Student's t distribution.
meanCi.fpcNote: Finite population correction applied if N is specified and less than 999,999,999.
meanCi.principleText: This module reproduces OpenEpi's MeanCI tool. It calculates the confidence interval for a mean from sample statistics. Two methods are provided: normal approximation (Z) and Student's t distribution, more suitable for small samples. A finite population correction can be applied if the total population size is known.
meanCi.zVsT: Z: large sample (n ≥ 30). t: small sample (n < 30) or unknown σ.
meanCi.fpcDesc: Reduces the standard error when the sample covers a significant portion of the population.
meanCi.zDesc: CI = x̄ ± z·σ/√n, where z is the quantile of the normal distribution. Usable for n ≥ 30 or known σ.
meanCi.tDesc: CI = x̄ ± t·s/√n, with t Student quantile at (n-1) df. Recommended in practice.

medianCi.title: Confidence Interval for the Median / Percentile
medianCi.description: Estimation of the percentile rank in the population
medianCi.copyPrefix: Confidence interval for the percentile - OpenEpi MedianCI
medianCi.interpretationText: At {{level}}% confidence, the {{percentile}}th percentile corresponds to ranks {{lowerRank}} to {{upperRank}} in a sorted sample of size {{n}}.
medianCi.howToUseSteps:
1. Sort your {{n}} observations in ascending order.
2. The {{lowerRank}}th value is the lower bound of the CI.
3. The {{upperRank}}th value is the upper bound.
At {{level}}% confidence, the true {{percentile}}th percentile lies between these two values.
medianCi.referenceNote: Method based on normal approximation of ranks (Conover, 1999).
medianCi.openEpiNote: Compliant with OpenEpi - Median/Percentile CI module.
medianCi.helpPrincipleText: This module reproduces OpenEpi's Median/Percentile CI tool. It estimates the confidence interval for the rank of a percentile in the population, from the sample size. Normal approximation of the binomial distribution is used.
medianCi.rankIntervalDesc: The interval is given as a position in the sorted sample, not directly as a value.
medianCi.conditionDesc: The approximation is valid if n·p ≥ 5 and n·(1-p) ≥ 5.
medianCi.intervalFormula: Interval — [max(1, floor(rank - z·SE)) , min(n, ceil(rank + z·SE))]

twoSampleTTest.title: Two Independent Sample t-Test
twoSampleTTest.description: Comparison of two means — Student's t-test, Welch's test, equality of variances test
twoSampleTTest.variancesDifferentAdvice: → Variances are significantly different (p < 0.05). Prefer Welch's test.
twoSampleTTest.variancesHomogeneousAdvice: → Variances are not significantly different (p ≥ 0.05). Student's test is appropriate.
twoSampleTTest.interpretationText: At {{level}}% confidence, the mean difference is {{diff}}.
twoSampleTTest.references: Student's t-test (equal variances) and Welch's t-test (Satterthwaite).
twoSampleTTest.fTestRef: Equality of variances test: Hartley's F (variance ratio).
twoSampleTTest.helpPrincipleText: This module reproduces OpenEpi's Two Sample T-Test tool. It compares the means of two independent groups from their sizes, means and standard deviations. Two versions of the t-test are provided: with or without the assumption of equal variances. The equality of variances test (Hartley's F) allows choosing the appropriate method.
twoSampleTTest.studentMethodDesc: pooled variance, confidence interval based on t distribution with n₁+n₂-2 df.
twoSampleTTest.welchMethodDesc: Welch standard error, Satterthwaite df, corresponding confidence interval.
twoSampleTTest.fMethodDesc: F = s₁²/s₂² (largest at numerator). Two-sided p-value.

oneWayAnova.title: One-Way ANOVA
oneWayAnova.description: Analysis of variance, Fisher's F test, Bartlett's test
oneWayAnova.minGroupsError: At least two groups are required
oneWayAnova.enterData: Enter at least two valid groups
oneWayAnova.fInterpret: Between-group variance is greater than within-group variance.
oneWayAnova.pInterpret: At least one mean differs significantly.
oneWayAnova.ssDescText: SSB = Σ nᵢ (x̄ᵢ - x̄)², SSW = Σ (nᵢ-1)·sᵢ², SST = SSB + SSW.
oneWayAnova.bartlettDesc: Based on the logarithm of variances, follows a χ² distribution.
oneWayAnova.ciDesc: CI with own variance (t, nᵢ-1 df) and CI with pooled variance (t, N-k df).
oneWayAnova.helpPrincipleText: This module reproduces OpenEpi's ANOVA tool. It compares the means of several independent groups from their sizes, means and standard deviations. The ANOVA table decomposes total variability into between-group and within-group variability, and provides Fisher's F test. Bartlett's test verifies equality of variances.

sampleSizeProportion.title: Sample Size for a Proportion
sampleSizeProportion.subtitle: Calculates the sample size needed to estimate a proportion (OpenEpi)
sampleSizeProportion.pNote: Between 0 and 99.99. If in doubt, use 50%
sampleSizeProportion.enterData: Enter valid data to see results
sampleSizeProportion.footnote: Calculation with finite population correction. p and d are expressed as percentages.
sampleSizeProportion.reportFooter: OpenEpi-style sample size calculator · jStat · autoTable
sampleSizeProportion.helpPrinciple: This calculator determines the sample size needed to estimate a proportion with a given absolute precision, using the finite population correction. It follows OpenEpi methodology and provides sizes for multiple confidence levels.
sampleSizeProportion.helpFormulaZ: Z = bilateral normal quantile for the chosen confidence level
sampleSizeProportion.helpUsage: Enter the population size (use 1,000,000 for "infinite"), the expected frequency (p), the desired absolute precision (± d) and the design effect (1 for simple random sampling). The table displays the required sample sizes for confidence levels from 80% to 99.99%.

unmatchedCaseControl.title: Sample Size - Unmatched Case-Control
unmatchedCaseControl.subtitle: Calculates sample size for an unmatched case-control study (Kelsey, Fleiss, Fleiss with CC)
unmatchedCaseControl.orNote: Provide either the odds ratio or the % of exposed cases; the other will be calculated automatically.
unmatchedCaseControl.enterDataHint: Check that % of exposed controls is between 0 and 100, ratio > 0, and odds ratio > 0 or % of exposed cases between 0 and 100.
unmatchedCaseControl.interpretationText: The sizes above are calculated to detect the specified odds ratio with the chosen power and confidence level. The Kelsey method uses normal approximation with pooled variance; Fleiss uses a more precise variance formula; Fleiss with continuity correction adjusts bias for small samples. The largest size among methods is often the safest choice.
unmatchedCaseControl.references: Kelsey et al., Methods in Observational Epidemiology 2nd Ed., Table 12-15; Fleiss, Statistical Methods for Rates and Proportions, formulas 3.18 & 3.19.
unmatchedCaseControl.footnote: CC = continuity correction. Results are rounded up. The Fleiss formula with correction uses the unrounded Fleiss sample size as a base.
unmatchedCaseControl.reportFooter: OpenEpi-style sample size calculator · jStat · autoTable
unmatchedCaseControl.helpPrinciple: This calculator estimates the sample size required for an unmatched case-control study. You specify the expected exposure proportion in controls (p0), the desired odds ratio (or proportion in cases), the controls/cases ratio (r), power and confidence level. Sizes are calculated using three common methods: Kelsey (normal approximation), Fleiss (exact variance) and Fleiss with continuity correction.
unmatchedCaseControl.helpUsage: Enter the confidence level (95% by default), power (typical 80%), controls/cases ratio (1 for equal groups), percentage of exposed controls (from the literature), and either the odds ratio to detect or the percentage of exposed cases. Results update automatically. Use the "Example" button to load typical values. You can copy results or export them to PDF.

cohortSampleSize.title: Sample Size - Cohort / Randomized Trial
cohortSampleSize.subtitle: Calculates sample size for a cohort study or randomized trial (Kelsey, Fleiss, Fleiss with CC)
cohortSampleSize.orNote: Enter one of the three (OR, RR or RD); the others will be calculated automatically.
cohortSampleSize.enterDataHint: Check that % of unexposed is between 0 and 100, ratio > 0, and one of the parameters (OR, RR or RD) is valid.
cohortSampleSize.interpretationText: The sizes above are calculated to detect the specified effect (OR, RR or RD) with the chosen power and confidence level. The Kelsey method uses normal approximation with pooled variance; Fleiss uses a more precise variance formula; Fleiss with continuity correction adjusts bias for small samples. The largest size among methods is often the safest choice.
cohortSampleSize.references: Kelsey et al., Methods in Observational Epidemiology 2nd Ed., Table 12-15; Fleiss, Statistical Methods for Rates and Proportions, formulas 3.18 & 3.19.
cohortSampleSize.footnote: CC = continuity correction. Results are rounded up. The Fleiss formula with correction uses the unrounded Fleiss sample size as a base.
cohortSampleSize.reportFooter: OpenEpi-style sample size calculator · jStat · autoTable
cohortSampleSize.helpPrinciple: This calculator estimates the sample size required for a cohort study or randomized trial. You specify the proportion of events in unexposed (p0), the desired effect (OR, RR or RD), the unexposed/exposed ratio (r), power and confidence level. Sizes are calculated using three common methods: Kelsey (normal approximation), Fleiss (exact variance) and Fleiss with continuity correction.
cohortSampleSize.helpUsage: Enter the confidence level (95% by default), power (typical 80%), unexposed/exposed ratio (1 for equal groups), percentage of unexposed with the event, and one of the three effect parameters (OR, RR or RD). Results update automatically. Use the "Example" button to load typical values. You can copy results or export them to PDF.

meanDiffSample.title: Sample Size - Mean Difference
meanDiffSample.subtitle: Calculates the sample size needed to detect a difference between two independent means
meanDiffSample.enterData: Enter valid parameters to see results
meanDiffSample.enterDataHint: Check that means and standard deviations are numbers, standard deviations > 0, ratio > 0 and means are different.
meanDiffSample.reportFooter: OpenEpi-style sample size calculator · jStat · autoTable
meanDiffSample.interpretationText: The calculated sample sizes give the study a power of {{power}}% to detect a difference of {{delta}} between the two means, with a confidence level of {{confidence}}% (two-sided test). The total size is {{total}}. The pooled standard deviation is a weighted average of the standard deviations of the two groups.
meanDiffSample.formula:
n₁ = (z_α/₂ + z_β)² × (σ₁² + σ₂² / r) / Δ²
n₂ = r × n₁
with Δ = |μ₁ - μ₂|, r = n₂/n₁
meanDiffSample.formulaExplanation: where Δ = |μ₁ - μ₂|, r = n₂/n₁, and z are quantiles of the normal distribution.
meanDiffSample.footnote: Results are rounded up. The pooled standard deviation is calculated from the estimated sample sizes.
meanDiffSample.helpPrinciple: This calculator determines the sample size needed to detect a specified difference between two independent means, based on standard deviations, confidence level, power and allocation ratio (n₂/n₁). It uses the standard formula for two independent samples (unequal variances).
meanDiffSample.helpUsage: Enter the means and standard deviations for both groups, confidence level (usually 95%), power (often 80%) and sample size ratio (n₂/n₁). Use the Example button to load typical values. Results display automatically. You can copy results or export them to PDF.

randomNumberGenerator.title: Random Number Generator
randomNumberGenerator.subtitle: Generates random numbers as in OpenEpi Random.
randomNumberGenerator.notesText:
Print numbers from your browser's File menu or copy and paste them into word processors, Excel and other programs.

Numbers are generated by the JavaScript Math.random() function. Although these are pseudo-random numbers, the Math.random function in common browsers has been tested by many people and found to generate high-quality 'random' numbers. For more information, search the Internet for 'quality random numbers' and related topics.
randomNumberGenerator.errorMinMax: Minimum must be less than or equal to maximum
randomNumberGenerator.errorUniqueRange: The requested count exceeds the available range for unique numbers
randomNumberGenerator.helpPrinciple: This generator produces random integers within a user-defined range. The 'Unique numbers' option allows sampling without replacement, useful for samples without repetition.
randomNumberGenerator.helpUnique: each number appears at most once (requires that the requested count ≤ range size).
randomNumberGenerator.helpMethod: Generation uses JavaScript's Math.random(), which produces a uniform pseudo-random value between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). For integers, the standard formula is applied:
randomNumberGenerator.helpReference: This tool is inspired by the 'Random Numbers' module of the OpenEpi epidemiological software. For more information on random number generation in JavaScript, see the
